questions.question: A seller of custom consulting hours knows each client's exact willingness to pay. Market demand is P = 200 - 2Q and MC = 40. Under first-degree price discrimination, what quantity is sold and what total variable surplus is captured by the firm?
questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination sells every unit for which willingness to pay is at least MC. Set 200 - 2Q = 40, so Q = 80. Captured surplus is 0.5(200 - 40)(80) = 6,400. B uses the ordinary monopoly quantity. C halves the surplus incorrectly. D combines monopoly quantity with the efficient-output surplus.

questions.question: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist faces P = 90 - Q and cost C(Q) = 10Q. What output is chosen, and how much consumer surplus remains?
questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination produces the efficient output where P = MC: 90 - Q = 10, so Q = 80. Because each buyer is charged their willingness to pay, consumer surplus is fully extracted and equals zero. A uses uniform monopoly output and ordinary consumer surplus. C uses monopoly output but zero consumer surplus. D uses efficient output but incorrectly leaves consumer surplus with buyers.

questions.question: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist faces P = 120 - Q. Marginal cost falls from 40 to 20. What happens to output and captured surplus?

choices.A: Output rises from 80 to 100 and captured surplus rises from 3,200 to 5,000 because more low-value units now have willingness to pay above MC.
choices.B: Output rises from 40 to 50 and captured surplus rises from 1,600 to 2,500 because it still sets MR = MC.
choices.C: Output stays at 80 because price discrimination only changes surplus division.
choices.D: Output falls from 100 to 80 because lower MC lowers willingness to pay.

questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination uses P = MC for the last unit. With MC = 40, Q = 80 and surplus = 0.5(80)(80) = 3,200. With MC = 20, Q = 100 and surplus = 0.5(100)(100) = 5,000. B uses uniform monopoly MR = MC. C ignores the cost change. D reverses the effect.

questions.question: A firm initially cannot identify individual willingness to pay and charges a uniform monopoly price. It then gains perfect information and can use first-degree price discrimination. Demand is P = 100 - Q and MC = 20. What changes?

choices.A: Output rises from 40 to 80, consumer surplus falls to zero, and total surplus rises because the efficient units are served.
choices.B: Output falls from 80 to 40, consumer surplus rises, and total surplus falls.
choices.C: Output stays at 40, but consumer surplus becomes zero.
choices.D: Output rises from 40 to 80, and consumer surplus rises because price falls to marginal cost for every buyer.

questions.explanation: Uniform monopoly gives Q = 40. Perfect price discrimination gives efficient output Q = 80, captures all surplus, and eliminates deadweight loss. B reverses output and surplus effects. C ignores efficient expansion. D confuses marginal-unit pricing with all buyers paying MC.

questions.question: On a demand-MC diagram for first-degree price discrimination, demand is downward sloping and MC is horizontal. Which area becomes producer surplus?

choices.A: The entire area under demand and above MC from zero to the efficient quantity.
choices.B: Only the rectangle between monopoly price and MC up to monopoly quantity.
choices.C: Only the triangle above monopoly price and below demand.
choices.D: No area, because price discrimination eliminates profit.

questions.explanation: A perfectly price-discriminating monopolist charges each buyer their willingness to pay, so it captures the entire surplus above MC up to the efficient quantity. B describes uniform monopoly producer surplus. C describes consumer surplus under uniform pricing. D is false.

questions.question: A graph shows ordinary monopoly output Qm = 30 and competitive output Qc = 60. Under first-degree price discrimination with the same demand and MC curves, which output is chosen and what happens to the deadweight-loss triangle?

choices.A: Q = 30; the deadweight-loss triangle remains but becomes producer surplus.
choices.B: Q = 60; the deadweight-loss triangle disappears because all units with willingness to pay above MC are produced.
choices.C: Q = 60; the deadweight-loss triangle becomes consumer surplus.
choices.D: Q = 90; output exceeds the efficient level because the firm can charge every buyer differently.

questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination expands output to the efficient quantity, eliminating the deadweight loss from restricted output. A keeps ordinary monopoly quantity. C misallocates the former DWL; it is created as additional surplus captured by the firm. D incorrectly predicts overproduction beyond P = MC.

questions.question: Why is first-degree price discrimination allocatively efficient but often viewed as unfavorable to consumers?

choices.A: It produces all units with willingness to pay above MC, but transfers all consumer surplus to the seller.
choices.B: It restricts output below the monopoly level but gives consumers lower prices.
choices.C: It sets one uniform price equal to average cost.
choices.D: It creates deadweight loss by charging low-value consumers too little.

questions.explanation: Perfect price discrimination is efficient because Q reaches the P = MC level. However, buyers receive no consumer surplus because each pays their maximum willingness to pay. B reverses the output effect. C describes average-cost pricing. D misunderstands the pricing rule.

questions.question: A car dealer knows each buyer's exact reservation price and can prevent resale. It negotiates a separate take-it-or-leave-it price with every buyer. Which model best applies?

choices.A: First-degree price discrimination, because each buyer is charged their maximum willingness to pay.
choices.B: Second-degree price discrimination, because buyers choose among quantity bundles.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination, because buyers are divided into observable groups only.
choices.D: Perfect competition, because negotiation eliminates market power.

questions.explanation: The key features are perfect information about individual willingness to pay and individualized prices, which define first-degree price discrimination. B involves self-selection among menus. C involves group-based pricing. D ignores the seller's market power and individualized extraction.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes first-degree price discrimination from ordinary monopoly pricing?

choices.A: First-degree price discrimination charges each unit according to willingness to pay and produces where P = MC; ordinary monopoly charges one price and produces where MR = MC.
choices.B: First-degree price discrimination charges one price and produces where MR = MC; ordinary monopoly charges each buyer a different price.
choices.C: Both produce the same output, but first-degree price discrimination has lower fixed cost.
choices.D: Ordinary monopoly eliminates consumer surplus, while first-degree price discrimination creates deadweight loss.

questions.explanation: First-degree price discrimination extracts individual surplus and expands output to the efficient level. Ordinary monopoly restricts output using MR = MC under a single price. B reverses definitions. C ignores output differences. D reverses the welfare properties.

questions.question: Demand is P = 50 - Q and MC = 10. Compare ordinary monopoly with first-degree price discrimination. What is the change in total surplus when the firm switches to first-degree price discrimination?

choices.C: Total surplus is unchanged, but consumer surplus falls to zero.

questions.explanation: Ordinary monopoly: MR = 50 - 2Q = 10 gives Qm = 20 and Pm = 30. Efficient output is Qc = 40. The monopoly DWL is 0.5(40 - 20)(30 - 10) = 200. First-degree price discrimination eliminates this DWL, so total surplus rises by 200. B reverses the sign. C ignores DWL elimination. D confuses total efficient surplus with the change.
